examples.title: Basic Merge Sort Implementation
examples.description: Demonstrates the classic divide-and-conquer merge sort algorithm with recursive splitting and merging of subarrays
examples.code:
def merge_sort(arr):
    if len(arr) <= 1:
        return arr
    
    # Divide: find middle point
    mid = len(arr) // 2
    left_half = merge_sort(arr[:mid])
    right_half = merge_sort(arr[mid:])
    
    # Conquer: merge sorted halves
    return merge(left_half, right_half)

def merge(left, right):
    sorted_array = []
    i = j = 0
    
    # Compare and merge elements
    while i < len(left) and j < len(right):
        if left[i] <= right[j]:
            sorted_array.append(left[i])
            i += 1
        else:
            sorted_array.append(right[j])
            j += 1
    
    # Append remaining elements
    sorted_array.extend(left[i:])
    sorted_array.extend(right[j:])
    return sorted_array

# Test the implementation
sample_array = [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10, 55, 21]
print("Original Array:", sample_array)
sorted_array = merge_sort(sample_array)
print("Sorted Array:", sorted_array)
examples.explanation:
1. Base case: if array has 0 or 1 element, it's already sorted
2. Divide: split array into two halves recursively
3. Conquer: merge two sorted halves by comparing elements
4. The merge function maintains sorted order by comparing elements from both halves
5. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to dividing into log n levels, each requiring O(n) merging

examples.title: Basic Quick Sort Implementation
examples.description: Demonstrates quick sort with Lomuto partition scheme and last element as pivot
examples.code:
def quick_sort(arr, low, high):
    if low < high:
        # Partition the array and get pivot index
        pivot_index = partition(arr, low, high)
        
        # Recursively sort elements before and after partition
        quick_sort(arr, low, pivot_index - 1)
        quick_sort(arr, pivot_index + 1, high)
    return arr

def partition(arr, low, high):
    # Choose last element as pivot
    pivot = arr[high]
    i = low - 1  # Index of smaller element
    
    for j in range(low, high):
        if arr[j] <= pivot:
            i += 1
            arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]
    
    # Place pivot in correct position
    arr[i + 1], arr[high] = arr[high], arr[i + 1]
    return i + 1

# Test the implementation
sample_array = [10, 7, 8, 9, 1, 5]
print("Original Array:", sample_array)
quick_sort(sample_array, 0, len(sample_array) - 1)
print("Sorted Array:", sample_array)
examples.explanation:
1. Choose a pivot element (last element in this implementation)
2. Partition: rearrange so elements smaller than pivot are on left, greater on right
3. The pivot is now in its final sorted position
4. Recursively apply quick sort to left and right subarrays
5. Average time complexity O(n log n), worst case O(n²) when array is already sorted
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst

examples.title: Practical Application: Student Record Sorting
examples.description: Advanced example showing practical use of both merge sort and quick sort for sorting complex data structures with performance comparison
examples.code:
import random
import time

# Student record class
class Student:
    def __init__(self, roll_no, name, marks):
        self.roll_no = roll_no
        self.name = name
        self.marks = marks
    
    def __repr__(self):
        return f"{self.roll_no}: {self.name} ({self.marks})"

# Merge Sort for student records (by marks)
def merge_sort_students(arr):
    if len(arr) <= 1:
        return arr
    
    mid = len(arr) // 2
    left = merge_sort_students(arr[:mid])
    right = merge_sort_students(arr[mid:])
    
    return merge_students(left, right)

def merge_students(left, right):
    result = []
    i = j = 0
    
    while i < len(left) and j < len(right):
        if left[i].marks <= right[j].marks:
            result.append(left[i])
            i += 1
        else:
            result.append(right[j])
            j += 1
    
    result.extend(left[i:])
    result.extend(right[j:])
    return result

# Quick Sort for student records
def quick_sort_students(arr, low, high):
    if low < high:
        pivot_index = partition_students(arr, low, high)
        quick_sort_students(arr, low, pivot_index - 1)
        quick_sort_students(arr, pivot_index + 1, high)

def partition_students(arr, low, high):
    pivot = arr[high].marks
    i = low - 1
    
    for j in range(low, high):
        if arr[j].marks <= pivot:
            i += 1
            arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]
    
    arr[i + 1], arr[high] = arr[high], arr[i + 1]
    return i + 1

# Generate test data
students = [Student(i, f"Student_{i}", random.randint(50, 100)) 
            for i in range(1, 501)]

# Test Merge Sort
students_copy1 = students.copy()
start = time.time()
sorted_merge = merge_sort_students(students_copy1)
merge_time = time.time() - start

# Test Quick Sort
students_copy2 = students.copy()
start = time.time()
quick_sort_students(students_copy2, 0, len(students_copy2) - 1)
quick_time = time.time() - start

print("=== Student Records Sorting Demo ===")
print(f"Number of records: {len(students)}")
print(f"Merge Sort Time: {merge_time:.6f} seconds")
print(f"Quick Sort Time: {quick_time:.6f} seconds")
print("\nTop 5 Students by Marks:")
for s in sorted_merge[-5:][::-1]:
    print(f"  {s}")
print("\nBottom 5 Students by Marks:")
for s in sorted_merge[:5]:
    print(f"  {s}")
examples.explanation:
This advanced example demonstrates practical algorithm application:1. Both algorithms can sort complex data structures by implementing custom comparison
2. Merge Sort: Stable sort, guaranteed O(n log n), uses O(n) extra space - better for linked lists
3. Quick Sort: In-place, faster in practice, cache-friendly but unstable
4. The code generates 500 random student records and compares sorting performance
5. In practice, Python's built-in sort (Timsort) often outperforms both for real-world data
6. Quick sort excels when memory is limited; merge sort preferred for external sorting
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for merge sort, O(log n) for quick sort
